data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper: A Beverage Portfolio Worthy Of Entry (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP) reported the company’s Q1 results on the 23rd of April. The beverage giant’s performance is split between a very strong refreshment beverage portfolio and a temporarily much weaker performance in the coffee business amid price volatility.

I believe that as coffee-related volatility should start to subside, and the market seems to price significant uncertainty regarding Keurig Dr Pepper’s separation into two pure-play companies, there is now an attractive entry point in the stock.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous October 2025 article on the stock, titled “Keurig Dr Pepper: The Focus Is On JDE Peet's Integration.” The stock has since returned 5%, nearly on par with the S&P 500’s 6% return.

Keurig Dr Pepper Q1 Review

On the surface, Keurig Dr Pepper’s Q1 results were stable. Revenues came in at $3.98 billion, reflecting fairly good 8.1% constant currency revenue growth. Growth stayed consistent from 8.6% in 2025. The quarter’s $0.39 adjusted EPS declined by -7.1% year-on-year but still came close to market expectations. Both revenues and the EPS beat Wall Street’s consensus estimates slightly.

The underlying performance was again much more mixed; earnings growth was split between a great performance in U.S. refreshment beverages and a much slower performance in U.S. coffee and international markets.

As said, refreshment beverages clearly remain a bright spot for Keurig Dr Pepper. The segment’s sales grew by 11.9% to $2.60 billion, reflecting very good volume growth of 7.2% as well as 4.7% growth from pricing increases. Keurig Dr Pepper’s soft beverage brand portfolio has clearly continued to capture U.S. market share; The Coca-Cola Company’s (KO) latest earnings reported much slower 1% unit case volume growth in North America, PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) reported a -2.5% organic North American beverage volume decline, and National Beverage Corp.'s (FIZZ) latest earnings reported a -0.3% revenue decline. All major peers have fared in comparison to Keurig Dr Pepper’s growth.

Adjusted operating income for the segment grew by 9.8% to $742 million, still reflecting some margin pressure. The adjusted operating margin declined by 60 basis points to 28.5%, as higher costs from aluminum tariffs and other cost inflation weighed on the industry. At the same time, Keurig Dr Pepper’s market share gains have required higher marketing expenses from the company.

Overall, I believe that a strong quarter sets up a confident outlook for Keurig Dr Pepper’s refreshment beverages despite a slight margin hiccup.

The coffee market has been much more challenging. The previous surge in coffee futures (KC1:COM) has raised costs immensely, pushing Keurig Dr Pepper to try to balance between margins and consumer demand when deciding price increases. There’s clear elasticity in consumer demand, leading to much less coffee consumption as prices have increased.

The Q1 report's financials still echoed coffee price turbulence. Keurig Dr Pepper’s U.S. coffee sales declined by -2.3% to $857 million, as a 5.9% pricing increase was met by an -8.2% decline in sales volume. Despite significant price hikes, the segment’s adjusted operating income declined by -21.3% to $199 million due to input cost inflation and other smaller factors. The adjusted operating margin declined by 560 basis points to 23.2%.

Luckily, the coffee segment’s financial performance should improve going forward. Coffee prices have moderated during late 2025 and early 2026, which should gradually start to show up in results as cheaper inventory starts to be sold. I don’t expect the U.S. coffee segment’s recovery to be instant, but a good earnings rebound should still happen with a delay. Keurig Dr Pepper needs to first cycle through more expensive inventory.

The JDE Peet's acquisition, completed on the 1st of April, should bring further potential to the coffee business. Keurig Dr Pepper targets $400 million in cost synergies as the businesses are integrated. The acquisition brings clear benefits from increased scale.

Finally, the international segment showed quite a mixed performance; sales grew by 8.5% in constant currency to $520 million due to pricing increases, but adjusted operating income declined by -15.1% to $87 million. Cost inflation and higher marketing expenses pressured the segment’s margins as Keurig Dr Pepper is trying to grow internationally. Similar coffee price concerns weighed on Keurig Dr Pepper’s international performance.

Overall, the report sets up good expectations for the rest of 2026. Keurig Dr Pepper reaffirmed the company’s 2026 guidance, suggesting revenues of $25.9-26.4 billion. The revenue guidance implies good 4-6% organic growth, as well as an $8.5-8.7 billion contribution from JDE Peet’s. The adjusted EPS is guided to grow in the low double-digit percentages, reflecting 4-6% organic growth and a 6-7 percentage point contribution from JDE Peet’s. Margins are guided to stabilize after a weaker Q1.

KDP Stock Seems Undervalued

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Keurig Dr Pepper. I now consolidate JDE Peet’s earnings from Q2’2026 forward as the company was acquired and add the company’s cash and debt as of December 2025.

I expect revenue growth to be very high in 2026 and 2027 due to JDE Peet’s acquisition, but I also expect fair mid-single-digit organic growth underneath in the next few years. Sales synergies with JDE Peet’s and a strong refreshment beverage performance create a platform for elevated growth. I still ultimately expect growth to gradually slow down to just 1.5% alongside inflation.

Cost synergies with JDE Peet’s and declining coffee prices should raise margins in the next few years. I expect the EBIT margin to reach 18% in a few years.

I don’t assume Keurig Dr Pepper needs to invest too much to achieve the estimated level of growth; the company’s after-tax cash flow conversion should remain fairly good.

The estimates put Keurig Dr Pepper’s fair value at $41.5, suggesting 46% upside as of writing. The stock seems to be undervalued if earnings perform nearly as well as I expect—there's an attractive entry point in my opinion. Uncertainty regarding the JDE Peet’s acquisition and the following business separation seems to weigh too much on the market’s sentiment.

The forward adjusted P/E stands at only 11.7, compared to The Coca-Cola Company’s 23.1 P/E and PepsiCo’s 17.8 P/E.

The fair value estimate is up from $33.8 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 6.06%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I now estimate a 5.79% interest rate, being 150 basis points above the current 10-year bond yield. I expect debt to remain quite high at a 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.29% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.54. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 6.75% and the WACC at 6.06%.

Takeaway

Keurig Dr Pepper reported fairly good, but varying, Q1 results. The company has continued to gain market share in U.S. refreshment beverages, but coffee price turbulence continues to weigh on the second largest segment. While international sales growth has been strong, cost inflation and marketing investments weigh on margins in international markets.

Overall, there’s reason to be optimistic about Keurig Dr Pepper’s future; coffee prices have started to stabilize, the JDE Peet’s acquisition should bring synergies, and the refreshment beverage outlook is strong. As KDP stock now seems clearly undervalued, I upgrade my rating to Buy.

data.title: RTX Corporation: Patience Will Be Rewarded
data.description:
Most of my analysis is focused on the tech sector, and so it's not a huge surprise that I haven't yet covered RTX Corporation (RTX). However, with the world seemingly becoming an increasingly dangerous place due to wars in Ukraine and the Middle East, RTX has become too important of a company to ignore any further. While we're currently in an environment that should suit RTX well, you can see below that the stock has seen not-so-great performance since the start of March. The defense contractor reported their latest earnings just a couple of days ago, and so today I have decided to initiate coverage.

Below, it is shown that the geopolitical landscape and defense spending trends are providing tailwinds for RTX. Demand is strong, as is evident by the rising backlog. The time needed to scale their production capacity is currently probably the largest risk to their near-term performance. That said, the long-term growth story is stronger than it has been in some time since defense spending trends triggered by wars in recent years are unlikely to die down even if the conflicts themselves end. With capacity expected to increase substantially over the coming decade or so, I believe investors should still find a decent amount of opportunity in RTX even if the valuation isn't cheap. Therefore, I'm initiating the stock at a Buy rating.

Surging Tomahawk Orders

Before getting into their earnings results, let's take a brief look at how the Iran war may be providing tailwinds for RTX's business. At this point, it is no secret that the conflict in the Middle East has used up a significant part of the United States' Tomahawk inventories. A little while ago, it was reported that the Navy is planning to purchase 785 Tomahawk missiles in 2027, up sharply from this year's 55. This is clearly driven by the Pentagon's need to restock, and so the tailwinds from the Iran war are clear.

Furthermore, with all eyes on the Strait of Hormuz, the United States is now trying to clear the mines that were set in the key waterway. RTX's Common Uncrewed Surface Vessel is one of the most important technologies being deployed, as it tows an advanced floating sonar that sweeps the seafloor. As of now, RTX's solutions are playing an integral part in the United States' Middle East mission, and with the war having no obvious end in sight, the company's importance cannot be overstated.

Growth Actually Slowed

For 2026 Q1, RTX reported sales of $22.1 billion, up 9% YoY. This was a deceleration from the previous quarter, and while this may seem to contradict the discussion above, it is important to realize that the company can't simply increase their production overnight. The tailwinds are there, but they also aren't going to translate into immediate top-line growth acceleration. Still, RTX beat analyst expectations by over $600 million, and so things could have been much worse.

In organic terms, growth slowed from Q4's 14% to 10% as a signal that current business activity isn't the strongest. Do note that both their Collins Aerospace and Pratt & Whitney segments saw 10% organic growth, while Raytheon saw a 9% increase. The fact that Raytheon relatively underperformed shows that key Tomahawk tailwinds have yet to kick in as of now. That's not a surprise.

Backlog Is Growing

With tailwinds needing time to show up in their top-line results, their order backlog numbers could be more indicative of what's to come for RTX. As shown above, for commercial aerospace, their backlog jumped by 30% YoY to $162 billion, while for their defense business, there was an 18% increase. Both growth rates clearly outpace the top-line growth rate for the company as a whole to show that demand is basically outstripping supply right now. For their Raytheon segment, while the Q1 book-to-bill ratio may have been a little softer than desired, the rolling 12-month book-to-bill was reported to be 1.48. That is a sign that there's no shortage in demand.

Capacity Expansion

In response to this buildup of backlog, the company is pursuing higher production capacity. In their earnings presentation it is stated that they're "investing to ramp up critical munitions over the next decade" and of course that includes Tomahawk. In the nearer term, it was reported on April 21st that Pratt & Whitney Canada had opened their manufacturing facility in Morocco, and so that should help to address the demand for aircraft engines.

Separately, it was reported just yesterday (April 22nd) that Pratt & Whitney is investing $100 million in Poland to boost production and add advanced capabilities. This is aimed at meeting demand for their engines, including the GTF, F135, and F100. At the end of the day, strong demand is encouraging, but capacity is needed for tailwinds to become meaningful. At this point, RTX seems focused on boosting production in a prudent manner.

Profitability Looks Solid

Just briefly, before we move to the outlook, profitability seems to be in a respectable position for the company as a whole. Adjusted operating margin saw YoY expansion in each of their three segments to show that the business is one that's well run. Raytheon led the way with around 150 basis points of expansion. This strong profitability clearly showed up on their bottom line as adjusted EPS climbed 21% YoY and also beat expectations by $0.26.

Full-Year Outlook

After beating top and bottom line expectations for Q1, RTX raised their full-year outlook for adjusted sales and for adjusted EPS, and of course that is welcome news for investors. Their organic sales growth rate projection remains unchanged, however, and the 5% to 6% range is a considerable slowdown from 2025's 11% increase. This indicates that the company's capacity expansion will take time and that the backlog to revenue conversion will perhaps be slower than what investors desire.

For adjusted EPS, the new midpoint implies growth of 8%, and so at least margins should continue to be strong moving forward. Despite the rise of the top and bottom line outlook, it should be noted that they still guided below consensus. Therefore, it seems that analysts thought tailwinds would translate into growth more quickly.

No Problem Securing Contracts

At this point, RTX is having no problems securing contracts. For example, on April 18th, it was reported that they, along with Lockheed Martin (LMT), had been contracted to sell $12 billion in naval systems to Germany. This will, of course, add to their backlog. Even more recently, RTX's Raytheon won a $213 million contract to upgrade the systems on the United States' Zumwalt-class vessels. Demand is clearly not the problem right now for their growth.

While demand outstripping supply is a much better problem to have than the other way around, the limits to scaling quickly are probably the largest risk to RTX's growth right now. If the company could deliver Tomahawk missiles now, there is little question that the Pentagon would buy them without hesitation. Therefore, moving forward, investors should keep these limitations in mind even if tailwinds get increasingly strong.

Valuation Analysis

While a forward P/E of 26.29 is on the high side for a company that's classified as an industrial company, I would say that the valuation is quite reasonable and should still provide long-term investors with a decent amount of opportunity. The recent pullback in the multiple from above 30 could be a nice entry point.

As stated above, the number one risk for RTX right now is capacity constraints. However, over the long run, the company is working to increase their production, and so investors can expect better backlog-to-revenue conversion over time. With the backlog totaling $271 billion right now and full-year 2025 revenues only having been $88.6 billion, it is clear that their business will be robust for the foreseeable future. While conflicts like the Iran war will end at some point, there is little doubt that the trends of increased global defense spending will continue as instability is seemingly on the rise.

With the long-term setup being as it is, I view RTX's relatively premium valuation as justified. The company can't meet all the demand in the near term, but with the growth story now being at its most solid point in recent memory, there are reasons to believe that the risk/reward is still favorable now.

Conclusion

RTX's stock performance actually hasn't been all that great recently as investors start to realize that demand can't be met in an easy manner. While this is a factor that short-term investors or traders should recognize, I believe for long-term investors, the recent pullback in the valuation provides a nice entry point. As capacity expansion leads to revenue and EPS growth acceleration over time, the stock will naturally move higher to maintain reasonable levels in their valuation multiples. With that in mind, I'm initiating RTX at a Buy rating, as outperformance is likely for patient investors.

data.title: Ford: Prepare For A Challenging Year Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Just when things seemed to be gradually improving for Ford (F), the company's management has confirmed that its electric vehicle strategy is not working. The announcement comes after a brief period of relief for Ford's investors after the Q3 2025 report.

A major restructuring was announced, and yet the market's response was relatively muted up until late February and early March, when forecasts about automotive sales were announced and it became clear that Ford's EV sales are collapsing.

While macroeconomic conditions are influencing all automotive OEMs, Ford's investors are in a far worse position when compared to those who invested in General Motors (GM) or Toyota (TM). F stock is up by less than 6% for the past 5-year period, and after accounting for inflation in that period, real return is in negative territory.

For years now, F investors have been awaiting a proper turnaround by relying on the stock's ridiculously low valuation. Even when compared to GM and Toyota, Ford's stock stands out. The price-to-sales multiple of 0.27 could easily be considered excessively low.

On a non-GAAP forward earnings basis, Ford is priced at a single-digit multiple, which is a rare sight in today's equity market.

The problem is that recent events at the company have shown that even these low multiples might not be enough to shelter shareholders from a potential future disaster at the company.

Never-Ending Problems

Ford's full fiscal year 2025 results were largely in line with expectations, and the market did not react negatively to the significant cost charges throughout the period. While we should recognize the difficulties that all legacy OEMs face, it is still hard to forego the trend in Ford's operating earnings over the past decade or so.

Profitability has gone nowhere over the past decade, with 2025 marking the second year in this 10-year timeframe when Ford reported significant GAAP losses. The first time this happened in 2020, the whole economy faced major challenges around the pandemic lockdowns, but in 2025 the problem is company-specific.

Around $4bn of cost headwinds in 2025 were outside of Ford's management control, with the aluminum supplier Novelis experiencing a major fire in one of its plants and tariffs weighing further on costs.

(...) $2 billion headwind from Novelis fires and the net tariff impact of $2 billion. That's a $1 billion higher tariff impact than we communicated just in October due to the unexpected and late year change in tariff credits for auto parts

Source: Ford Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript

The impact of such cost items on share price performance is often limited within the short term, as market participants tend to disregard one-off cost headwinds.

On an adjusted basis, Ford's EV segment even appears to be doing quite well, with sales up significantly in FY 2025 and operating losses narrowing down.

The harsh reality, however, is that the management has recognized that its EV strategy is not working. A strategy that has been promoted for many years is now coming under scrutiny and would most likely result in sluggish sales, more losses in the coming years, and high restructuring costs.

The extract from Ford's most recent annual SEC filing speaks for itself and could be a precursor to years of automotive segment losses as the company pivoted heavily towards EVs.

The challenges facing the electric vehicle (“EV”) market that have led to lower-than-anticipated adoption rates have, in turn, led us to conclude, in the fourth quarter of 2025, that a path to long-term profitability for our EV business was not possible without taking strategic actions. Accordingly, in December 2025, we made the decision to rationalize our EV manufacturing capacity and product roadmap by cancelling three previously planned EVs and ending production of the current generation F-150 Lightning EV.

As a result of the challenges facing the EV market and the decisions we made in response to those challenges, in the fourth quarter of 2025, we tested our Model e segment long-lived assets for impairment and recorded a pre-tax charge of $8.1 billion in Cost of sales, representing the amount by which the carrying value of these assets exceeded the estimated fair value. We primarily used the market and cost approaches to estimate fair value for our long-lived assets.

Source: Ford 10-K SEC Filing

The aforementioned $8bn impairment charge was not all, with significant additional charges being realized in 2025 in relation to cancellations and contractual commitments also weighing on profitability.

In addition to the charge described above, in the fourth quarter of 2025, as part of Cost of sales, we recognized asset write-downs of $1.1 billion for assets related to the EV program cancellations referenced above; recognized $1.2 billion of other charges, primarily related to contractual commitments related to those programs; and fully impaired Model e segment goodwill of $0.2 billion

Source: Ford 10-K SEC Filing

To make matters worse, competition in the EV space is not sleeping and, in fact, seems to be intensifying. Just yesterday, Tesla (TSLA) reported its Q1 2026 results, and Elon Musk made clear that the company is entering into an investment cycle that will include a notable pickup in investments and capital expenditures.

All that is now likely to become a major threat for Ford going forward, as the company was already struggling with achieving high profitability in its automotive segment and relied heavily on Ford Credit to fill up the hole in its bottom line. Not to mention that Ford's restructuring and a potential shift in its EV strategy comes at a time when the U.S. consumer is coming under significant pressure and consumer confidence has collapsed to record low levels.

Expect Cash Flow Pressures

Even when we disregard the non-cash impairment charges in 2025, things still look very grim for Ford. In recent years, the company's cash flow from operations has been barely sufficient to cover the company's ongoing capital expenditure and dividend outlays. This has left very little leeway for more growth capex, strategic acquisitions, deleveraging, dividend increases, and share buybacks.

As we could see on the graph below, this trend seems to have changed in 2025, but unfortunately this is likely to be short-lived.

On the graph below, we once again have Ford's annual cash flow from operations plotted on an annual basis, but this time against the annual change in working capital.

As opposed to 2023 and 2024, when the overall impact of working capital was negligible, in 2025 we had a massive positive cash inflow due to changes in working capital. This was caused by a drop in finance receivables and a significant increase in accounts payable and other current liabilities (see the table below).

The lower amount of finance receivables is concerning when it comes to Ford's revenue growth and, in particular, the expansion of Ford Credit. The $10bn positive impact from higher accounts payable and other liabilities, on the other hand, is highly unlikely to be sustained if topline growth remains low. At present, Ford's expected revenue growth rate stands below 1%, which means that investors should anticipate a much lower working capital tailwind in 2026.

Conclusion

Although the market was prepared for the disappointing results in 2025, Ford's most recent earnings release has shown that investors should not anticipate a turnaround anytime soon. The upcoming Q1 earnings release next week will shed some light on what's in store for 2026, but a necessary change in EV strategy could mean a few more years of pain for Ford's shareholders. This comes at a time when the U.S. consumer will face significant pressures in spending and Ford's cash flow is likely to shrink in 2026. As a result, even the record low valuation multiples can't justify the risks involved in holding Ford's stock.

data.title: ON Semiconductor: Chip Stocks' Parabolic Rise Draws Concerns
data.description:
Semiconductor stocks are working on a perfect April (as of this writing). Trading higher for 17 days running, the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) reached a record winning streak as the AI-led bull market simply went into another gear. The early-2026 decline feels like a distant memory, and investors now await key chip-maker earnings.

One of these market leaders is ON Semiconductor (ON). The stock has soared by more than 75% since its March 30 low. The stock jumped 9% on Thursday, April 23, following an upgrade out of B. Riley, along with bullish trends in Intel (INTC) and (more so) a stunning post-reporting climb in Texas Instruments (TXN).

As for ON, I have a "Hold" rating. The valuation is not extreme, but it’s no longer a bargain. The chart, meanwhile, has gone dangerously parabolic, with the $111 record high not far above the current price.

ON & SMH Going Parabolic As Earnings Season Progresses, Software Slides

Back in February, ON reported a mixed set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.64 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.62, while revenue of $1.53 billion, down 12% from the same period last year, was a small $10 million miss. Its non-GAAP gross margin came in at 38.2%, while the operating margin verified at 19.8%.

Shares rose 3.5% in the session that followed (after an initial dip), which was actually the best earnings reaction going back to July 2024. Looking ahead to the May 4 Q1 report, the options market prices in a high 9.5% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Shares of the $35 billion market cap Semiconductors industry company feature extremely high implied volatility of 77.9%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, ON’s net sales dipped 1% sequentially, but revenue rose in its Automotive, Industrial, and AI Data Center segments. The company also tallied record free cash flow of $1.4 billion (a 24% FCF margin) and AI data center revenue exceeding $250 million. The management team noted progress in its expansion of the Treo platform, which reached a design-win funnel of over $1 billion. A large buyback shouldn't go unnoticed, given the authorization of a new $6 billion share repurchase program.

Color On Quarter

As for expectations for the Q1 report due out in just a couple of weeks, ON sees revenue ranging between $1.435 and $1.535 billion, along with a non-GAAP gross margin in the range of 37.5% and 39.5%. According to the Q1 presentations, its non-GAAP diluted EPS is forecast between $0.56 and $0.66.

The company's long-term outlook remains focused on high-growth secular trends, targeting a 10% to 12% revenue CAGR and a significant expansion of gross margins to 53%. Those are high bars to hit, so this upcoming quarterly report could be a bit of a show-me story after the big stock price ascent. Big picture, ON continues shedding non-core assets (something the B. Riley upgrade called out) to stabilize its core products. Planned exits could result in a $300 million headwind, so says BofA.

Q1 Guidance

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong over the years ahead. The 25%-38% bottom-line growth rate warrants a premium earnings multiple, even with ON’s position in a highly cyclical industry. Thus, I don’t think the stock is all that overvalued after the massive surge since late March.

The sell-side, meanwhile, has been net bullish on the Arizona-based large-cap, as evidenced by 17 upgrades in the past 90 days versus 11 downgrades. Finally, ON is highly profitable, as evidenced by $3.45 of free cash flow per share over the past year.

ON: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $3.25 of normalized non-GAAP per-share earnings over the next 12 months and apply a 30x price-to-earnings multiple, shares should trade near $97.50. That makes ON fairly priced today.

Consider that the implied PEG ratio is only about 1 with that P/E (based on growth trends). Shares do trade expensively on a price-to-sales basis, however.

ON: Mixed Valuation Metrics, Fair PEG

Key risks for ON include the possibility of a global macroeconomic slowdown, which would put pressure on the cyclical semiconductor industry. ON has high exposure away from AI-direct chip applications, such as its Automotive and Industrial segments.

Of course, ongoing trade tensions are not exactly a tailwind for this multinational corporation. For ON specifically, the onus is on the management team to navigate divestitures with acumen.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Monday, May 4 AMC. The company hosts its Annual General Meeting on Thursday, May 14.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares just about going parabolic in the last few weeks, ON’s technical situation is precarious. Notice in the chart below that its RSI momentum reading is within a couple of points of the highest in the stock’s history.

On the upside, the mid-2023 high of $111 is certainly in play, but we could see a sharp bearish reversal at any time, given implied volatility levels. What’s more, there are three key downside price gaps that linger, which suggests that some back-and-fill is possible ahead of the Q1 report in early May.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, which is a positive, but it’s also way down at $57. Hence, shares are extremely separated from that trend-indicator line. Furthermore, there are simply not many shares traded down to the mid-$80s. Support is near $75, and that would be an ideal spot to buy the dip before or after the May earnings event.

ON: Gapping Higher, Bearish Reversal Risk Increasing, Extreme RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a "Hold" rating on ON. The valuation is not extreme, but technicals augur for a pullback very soon, with the all-time high not far above today’s price. In the upcoming Q1 report, be on the lookout for updates on exit plans and its AI market strategy.

data.title: Booking Holdings: The Cracks Are Showing And It's Time To Buy Puts
data.description:
Booking Holdings Inc. (BKNG) just completed a 25-for-1 stock split, bringing the share price from above $4,000 down to roughly $170. The split made the stock more accessible to retail investors. BKNG closed on April 22nd at $179.40, down 6% after SEC filings revealed the CEO and a board director sold shares. Overall, the stock is down 18% year-to-date despite beating Q4 earnings estimates. That kind of price action after a beat tells you pretty much all you need to know about where institutional sentiment is heading.

I'm bearish, and I'm buying puts. Here's why.

The Story In The Chart

Before I get into the fundamental case, let me start with what first caught my eye: the gaps.

BKNG has two open area gaps below the current price. The first sits at $177.76, and the second at $168.11. Both were created during the post-split volatility as the stock gapped through these levels on its way up from the March lows. Neither has filled.

If you've read my gap-trading strategies, you know this reliable profit-maker: area gaps. They fill. My backtests across thousands of instances show fill rates above 85% for area gaps, with the median fill time being a little over seven trading days. These aren't gaps driven by a structural shift in the business (we call gaps of that sort "breakaway gaps"). No, these are area gaps that formed on the kind of noisy, news-driven volatility that generates temporary dislocations and temporary momentum that reverts.

The first gap, at $177.76, is my conservative target. That's a roughly 1% decline from the current price of $179, which barely qualifies as a move (though they can still be highly profitable if you employ option strategies), but what matters here is the signal: once the stock breaks into that gap, the statistical tendency is to fill it completely, and the momentum from filling the first gap often carries into the second. The $168.11 gap is my optimistic target, representing a move of roughly 6%. With earnings on April 28 acting as a potential catalyst and three separate negative headlines in the last ten days providing a fundamental backdrop, my Bayesian probability of both gaps filling over the next three to four weeks is elevated: Assuming the 85% average fill rate, we get .85*.85=.7225, or a 72.25% probability of both gaps filling, but my Bayesian bias (negative factors, which I will promptly describe below) pushes this higher, to, say, 80%. I.e., I believe with 80% confidence that BKNG will hit $168.11 in the coming weeks.

Most importantly, the technical picture and the fundamental picture are saying the same thing. That's the kind of alignment I look for before opening a trade.

A Growing Regulatory Problem

On April 22, Italy's antitrust authority launched an investigation into Booking.com, accusing the company of abusing its market dominance by imposing unfair terms on hotel partners. The investigation centers on allegations that Booking has "leveraged its significant market power to enforce 'most-favored nation' clauses, which restrict hotels from setting competitive prices on other platforms."

The stock dropped 6% on the news. And this isn't just an isolated incident; it's the latest in a pattern.

Booking is already appealing a 413 million euro fine in Spain for leveraging its 70-90% market share to impose most-favored-nation clauses on hotels and to restrict competitive pricing on rival platforms. The EU has designated Booking.com as a "gatekeeper" under the Digital Markets Act (hereafter "DMA"), subjecting it to stricter competition rules that could force costly operational changes. And now Italy is piling on.

Think of this like a game of regulatory whack-a-mole. Booking can appeal the Spanish fine, and it might win, but by the time that plays out, Italy has opened a new front. And the EU's DMA framework means the regulatory infrastructure for going after Booking is now permanent, not ad hoc. Every European market where Booking holds 70%+ of the shares is a potential investigation waiting to happen.

The financial exposure isn't trivial. The Spanish fine alone is nearly half a billion euros. Even if Booking wins on appeal, the legal costs, compliance costs, and potential business practice changes required under the DMA will drag down Booking's margins.

The Data Breach Nobody's Talking About

On April 13, Booking.com confirmed that hackers accessed customer booking data, including names, email addresses, phone numbers, and reservation details. The company forced PIN resets for existing and past reservations and notified affected users (including yours truly).

Microsoft attributed the attack to a criminal group called Storm-1865, which used ClickFix to compromise hotel partner accounts. The stolen data are exactly what you'd need to run convincing reservation hijacking scams, and users are already reporting being targeted by scammers using real booking information.

This isn't Booking's first rodeo with breaches. The company was fined 475,000 euros by Dutch regulators for late breach reporting back in 2021. The pattern of partner-account compromises suggests a systemic vulnerability in Booking's method of integration with its accommodation providers.

For a company whose entire value proposition is "trust us with your travel data and credit card," recurring security incidents are corrosive to the brand in ways that don't show up in the next quarter's numbers but absolutely show up in other ways. It's slow, compounding damage, like smoking (which I quit five years ago, and you should, too).

Insiders Are Selling And Nobody's Buying

SEC filings from the last two weeks show CEO Glenn Fogel sold 16,726 shares at $185.36 on April 15, reducing his position by 5.31%. Director Vanessa Ames Wittman sold 1,125 shares at $192 on April 17, a 6.55% reduction. Both transactions were executed under prearranged Rule 10b5-1 trading plans.

The 10b5-1 defense is the standard corporate line: "These were pre-planned sales; nothing to see here." And in isolation, that's true. But zoom out to see the pattern: Over the last six months, Booking insiders have made 199 sales and zero purchases. Not 199 sales and 3 purchases. Not 199 sales and 1 token buy. Zero total buys. And if you don't like that colorful English description, here's one from the mathematician part of me: The sell-to-buy ratio is infinite.

AI Disintermediation Is No Longer Theoretical

Here's the part that makes me most bearish on BKNG as a long-term hold and most confident that the stock hasn't finished declining.

The Harvard Business Review published a piece in January titled "Gen AI Is Threatening the Platforms That Dominate Online Travel." The thesis is simple: As conversational AI tools answer user queries directly and can access inventory in real time, traditional aggregator platforms such as Booking.com risk losing their gateway roles entirely.

This isn't just academic hand-wringing. The Hacker News developer community has been building AI travel tools at a striking pace. Browse the "Show HN" posts from the last six months, and you'll find AI itinerary builders that parse booking confirmations, chatbot-first travel planners, and direct hotel-to-AI booking pipelines that completely bypass online travel agencies. Many of these might seem like scrappy projects for now, but they represent the ultimate vector of disruption.

The industry press is aligned. Skift reported that Amazon, Meta, and Google are independently developing AI travel planning systems, each with its own architecture and partnerships. Sixty percent of PhocusWire's "Hot 25 Travel Startups" for 2026 are AI-native, up from single digits in prior years. Sam Altman himself has said travelers may soon book hotels in one click through ChatGPT, positioning it as an unbiased alternative to platforms that accept money in exchange for ranking results.

Booking's moat is its supply aggregation: 6.8 billion cumulative bookings since 2010, with millions of listed properties. But aggregation moats erode when the discovery layer moves somewhere else. If travelers start finding and booking hotels through AI assistants that access inventory directly, Booking's take rate, the backbone of its revenue model, will almost certainly be reduced, and the company's moat will be in danger.

Now, as you might expect, Booking is investing in AI internally, and CEO Glenn Fogel has talked about their "Connected Trip" vision and agentic AI tools that reduced cost per booking by about 10% in 2025. I find this rather silly: Booking is trying to use AI to make its existing platform more efficient, while AI is trying to make Booking's platform non-existent. That's like Blockbuster bragging to investors that they've been using Netflix sales data to best choose what movies to carry while Netflix itself builds a streaming service that will ultimately put Blockbuster out of business.

The Valuation Doesn't Compensate For The Risk

At $179, BKNG trades at roughly 29x trailing earnings and 18x forward estimates. The forward multiple looks reasonable until you consider what's most likely being priced into those forward estimates: mid-teens EPS growth, continued merchant model margin expansion, and no material impact from regulatory actions or AI disruption.

Room night growth has already decelerated. Q4 2025 room nights grew 9%, down from the double-digit clips of the recovery era. Revenue guidance for 2026 calls for low double-digit constant-currency growth, which sounds fine until you realize the stock was priced for accelerating growth when it was trading around $230. The market is already marking down the growth trajectory. The question is whether 18x forward is cheap enough or whether there's another leg down as growth continues to decline.

Free cash flow was $9.1 billion in 2025, and the company returned $7.6 billion to shareholders through $6.4 billion in buybacks and $1.2 billion in dividends. That's attractive. But the 413 million euro Spanish fine (which is, granted, on appeal), the Italian investigation, DMA compliance costs, and breach-related remediation are all potential draws on that cash flow that seemingly haven't been priced in yet.

Risks

Risk 1: Travel demand surprises to the upside. The FIFA World Cup 2026 is a massive global event, and Booking's exposure to European and Latin American markets positions it to capture significant incremental bookings. A strong summer travel season combined with World Cup demand could drive a Q2/Q3 beat that sends the stock higher.

Risk 2: The stock split draws sustained retail buying. Retail inflows after splits are common. BKNG below $200 is suddenly accessible to a whole new class of buyer, and if traders pile in, the stock could bounce regardless of fundamentals.

Risk 3: Regulatory outcomes turn out to be milder than expected. The Spanish fine could be reduced on appeal; the Italian probe could result in behavioral remedies rather than financial penalties; and the DMA could prove less disruptive than many fear. Notably, European regulatory outcomes frequently disappoint the bears.

The Play

I'm buying the May 16 $170 puts. At the current premiums, these should cost roughly $5 per contract. The thesis I outlined above leads me to believe that the stock has a high probability of a path to the first gap fill at $177.76 and then a path to the second gap at $168.11. I believe this will occur over the next three to four weeks.

If BKNG reports a clean Q1 on April 28 and raises guidance, these puts are likely to become worthless. That's the risk, and it's defined. But with 30 Buy ratings and zero Sells among covering analysts, the bar for a "good enough" earnings report is extremely high. Anything less than a beat-and-raise, especially with the Italian probe and breach as overhangs, is likely to be punished.

You could, of course, sell your position before earnings, which might actually be the actual defined play, should the stock hit our price targets before April 28. But if not, you could reasonably hold through earnings. If your risk tolerance is low, though, I recommend closing the trade before earnings instead of based on the price target defined by the gap.

Yet I'm not against holding through earnings, either. The setup reminds me of what happens when a stock splits at a top and then fails to hold: the new retail shareholders who bought the "cheaper" version of the stock become the weakest hands. When the next piece of bad news hits, they sell first, and they sell hard. BKNG has had three pieces of bad news in the last ten days. The next catalyst is earnings, and the market has already told you it doesn't believe in the bull case at current prices.

Overall, the risk is defined, and the catalysts are stacked. I like the short side here.

data.title: Beachbody: Impressive Turnaround, But Can It Continue?
data.description:
At its last close, nutrition supplements and fitness programs provider The Beachbody Company, Inc. (BODI), was at $14.4. This is just a little over 2% of its price at listing in 2021 and is a clear tell that things haven't gone well for the company, as evident in its financials.

But a turnaround is underway, and that has clearly impressed investors considering that BODI is up by 276% over the past year. First, a look at the restructuring program and its encouraging results so far. An outlook for the company is developed next, followed by a look at its market multiples.

Post-Pandemic Challenges...

When Beachbody got listed in 2021, the time seemed just right. It had done well in 2020, with 10% revenue growth, a 71% gross margin, and a positive adjusted EBITDA, even with an underwhelming margin of 6%.

But in 2021, signs of weakness already started showing. Revenue growth dwindled to just 1.1%, the gross margin dropped by almost 9 percentage points, and the company fell into a loss on an adjusted EBITDA basis. It was to be expected, though. As an online fitness solutions provider, its demand was affected in the post-lockdown world.

The downhill trajectory for the company in the succeeding years was more disappointing. By 2023, the company's revenues had dropped by 33% even from its pre-pandemic levels of 2019, and the gross margin was down by over 10 percentage points to 61.3%. To its credit, while the adjusted EBITDA loss margin shrank to just 1.7% from 9.9% in 2021, the fact remained that Beachbody was still making losses.

... Led to Dramatic Restructuring

To get a grip on the dramatic slip in performance, the company embarked on a restructuring program in September 2024. The key actions to undertake in the program were as follows:

Improving Financial Performance

The first phase of this restructuring's organization was to "centered on lowering our infrastructure costs and re-architecting our financial model," as per the company's press release. In line with this, by the time the program was revealed, Beachbody was already well underway to achieving its financial goals.

The level of revenues required to reach breakeven had already been reduced by more than $400 million through cost reductions in 2024. It next aimed to reduce the break-even revenue to less than $225 million.

Beachbody had also managed a turnaround in its adjusted EBITDA from Q4 2023 onwards, even though it reported a loss during the year. Annualized cost savings of some $54 million and a workforce reduction of 33% were also envisaged as part of the plan.

Change to Sales and Marketing Model

It also started its transition away from multi-level marketing [MLM], which it says faced sentimental backlash in the post-COVID-19 US market. Critics of the marketing strategy have pointed to its high failure rates, which gained much momentum over the years, as this Atlantic article from 2021 pointed out.

In any case, the company notes that the MLM model was "burdensome" since the commissions on sales were 40-50%. It has since moved to single-level affiliate programs as of November 2024. As an Amazon affiliate, it's now reaching consumers directly, along with partnerships with other sales channels.

Encouraging Outcomes

While the company still has some way to go in achieving a complete turnaround, the following outcomes demonstrate the encouraging outcomes seen so far.

  • Revenues: The gap in the revenue breakeven level continued to shrink, dropping to $235 million in 2024. In its plan, Beachbody had mentioned a goal of $225 million, so it's possible that the full target was mildly underachieved, but it doesn't mention the target date for achievement. The bigger point here is that the breakeven point continues to get closer as the figure dropped further to $180 million in 2025.

  • Costs: Costs reduced with a headcount drop of over 700 in the last four years. The company's operating expenses halved in 2025 from the year before, and sales and marketing expenses fell to 37.2% of revenues in 2025 (2023: 53.5%).

  • Profits: The company has sustained adjusted EBITDA profits for the past two years now, with an 8.8% increase in 2025. It also turned operating income positive last year, the first time since its public listing. A small adjusted net income was also observed during 2025 (see table below), and reported net income turned positive in H2 2025.

Debt Reduction and Refinancing

As a company still in the process of turnaround, it's particularly positive that Beachbody's debt levels are highly sustainable, meriting a special mention. After halving its debt with Blue Torch Capital to $25.5 million, the company also refinanced it with Tiger Finance at better terms in early 2024.

As a result, its gross interest coverage ratio has improved to 1.6x as of 2025 compared to 1.2x if the company had made the same operating profit in 2024. Also, its debt-to-EBITDA ratio has now dropped to a healthy under 1x.

Outlook and Market Multiples

However, the outlook is wanting. The table below provides the company's guidance for Q1 2026. If this is any reflection of the full year, I believe another pullback is in store. At the midpoint of the guidance range, the revenue would drop by 29% YoY, continuing the trend of softening revenues.

While a notable increase of 49% YoY is expected during the quarter in adjusted EBITDA, both at the adjusted and reported level, the company would still report a net loss. Analysts estimates on Seeking Alpha also confirm expectations of revenue contraction for the full year, though a small net profit is anticipated.

What's Next?

BODI's forward P/E is at a massive 106x right now, which improves to a far smaller 7.1x by 2027. For context, the forward P/E for the consumer discretionary sector is at 15.9x right now. A 22% revenue growth is also envisaged for next year, which brings the forward P/S to a low 0.39x (consumer discretionary sector, 2026: 0.92x).

However, at present, the company's numbers don't give enough assurance that the outlook will come through. A potential revenue contraction is disappointing enough, and if it's accompanied by losses as well, it would bring the turnaround into question. For this reason, it's best to wait and watch for now until Beachbody's recovery is on firmer footing. I'm going with a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sirius XM's Weird Space Rally Explained
data.description:
A Likely Outperformer

Sirius XM Holdings Inc. (SIRI) has been an extraordinarily satisfying holding in recent weeks, and although it is up 40% YTD, it is still very cheap, trading for just 8.45x forward EPS and sporting a 4.13% dividend yield.

The company has just concluded a multi-year investment cycle, is almost certain to deliver $1.35 billion of FCF in 2026 (15.3% FCF yield), and will resume substantial buybacks next year, when debt levels will have been reduced thanks to those FCF inflows.

Looking Like A Value Trap

That said, the chart is ugly. Even after its recent recovery, the stock has more than halved from its usual levels a few years ago and has burnt many longs by flip-flopping on its streaming strategy, (slowly) losing subscribers, and overall, the company looks like just another legacy media loser and a typical value trap.

Many short-lived rallies have come on its way down and lured in investors; there have been occasional short squeezes, but ultimately the stock has always resumed its downward trajectory.

Why Should This Rally Be Different?

Last year, when I called the bottom for the stock, I wrote about the increasing investor interest in Sirius XM’s spectrum assets:

Since Sirius XM (roughly speaking) owns about twice as much spectrum as it needs, there is a huge monetization opportunity. While an outright sale is complicated, probably not even smart to do (as it would create some technical and regulatory problems), the fastest way to make money from this asset is through third-party partnerships.

It looks like we will soon find out how such partnerships can unfold and add to Sirius XM’s bottom line.

Two weeks ago, right before the stock added 20% in a few days, the Federal Register released a Proposed Rule, “Spectrum Abundance for Weird Space Stuff,” the only weird detail of which is the title. Absent negative comments, the new rule will become effective on 5/11/26. Otherwise, by 6/8/26, reply comments are due.

In a lengthy document, the FCC (Federal Communications Commission) explains why it wants to expand Sirius XM’s rights concerning its spectrum to allow for additional uses for telemetry, tracking, and command (TT&C) functions that are essential for operating emergent spacecraft. ‘‘Emergent space operations’’ are defined as “those spacecraft or commercial operations in space that use radio spectrum for control of, or communications with, a spacecraft, but which are not communications satellites.”

The agency justifies its intervention by the current shortage of usable and readily accessible spectrum for the mentioned TT&C functions. Additionally, the document proposes to add a secondary allocation for SOS (Space Operation Service) in spectrum frequency bands such as Sirius XM's, which are used below their capacity in certain geographic areas. Finally, the new rule would allow the leasing of such spectrum to earth station licensees to provide SOS in connection with emergent spacecraft.

As the document explains,

Once the province of science fiction, American companies are now upgrading, relocating, and servicing satellites; manufacturing pharmaceuticals in space; building private inhabitable spacecraft; and conducting private robotic missions to the surface of the Moon. Emergent space operations like these depend on the use of radiocommunications for their spacecraft. … Spectrum is a critical component of all space operations. Even for spacecraft that do not provide radiocommunications services to the public, reliable spectrum access is mandatory … to control spacecraft in orbit. American innovators, however, currently face an acute shortage of usable and readily accessible spectrum for TT&C, and that spectrum crunch threatens to delay—or even prevent— the growth of domestic space technologies and jeopardize U.S. leadership in the booming global space economy. … For one, spectrum for TT&C is largely unavailable on a standalone basis, absent a separately authorized radiocommunications service. Emergent space innovations like inhabitable spacecraft and orbital laboratories, however, do not typically provide a separately authorized radiocommunications service. For another, the spectrum bands where TT&C may be available on a standalone basis tend to be congested and occupied primarily by Federal users.

Importantly, there is currently no domestic or international radiocommunication service specifically dedicated for use by emergent space operations. Consequently, the agency proposes

to add a secondary allocation for SOS (Earth-to-space) in the 2320–2345 MHz band in the non-Federal column of the United States Table, and to permit the existing exclusive licensee within this frequency band in the United States, SiriusXM, to lease portions of its spectrum to earth station licensees for command of spacecraft that do not otherwise provide radiocommunication services to the public. … Because TT&C functions are not always transmitting and employ narrowband signals, often 100 kilohertz in bandwidth or less, we anticipate that a large number of TT&C communications can be accommodated in the 25 megahertz of the 2320–2345 MHz band, particularly if multiple earth station uplink sites can be coordinated with SiriusXM.

Finally, the agency basically intends to transform Sirius XM into some sort of gatekeeper for emerging space operations and explicitly allows Sirius XM to be compensated for such efforts:

As proposed above, any earth station licensee that seeks to operate in the 2320–2345 MHz band would have to certify that its proposed operations have been coordinated with and approved by SiriusXM in order to avoid harmful interference to SiriusXM’s current and potential operations. … Such coordination is likely to require expenditure of resources by SiriusXM, or the forgoing of future rights to operate in certain areas, for the benefit of third party spectrum users. As such, we tentatively conclude it is reasonable for SiriusXM to require financial compensation for the expenditures under these conditions.

What Is It All Worth?

It is impossible to know the earnings potential from the proposed spectrum licensing.

Typically, spectrum licensing deals are priced based on population coverage, MHz, and technical specifics of the spectrum involved. Prices range from a few cents per MHz/POP to $2 per MHz/POP. For example, 1 MHz times 300 million people times $2 would result in an annual licensing fee of $600m.

That said, the spectrum licensing market is set to grow strongly over the next decade, as spectrum uses increase while spectrum availability is scarce. This means licensing fees are very likely to increase strongly over time.

In its recent 10-K, Sirius XM states a carrying value for its spectrum licenses of $8.6 billion.

The recent transaction between AT&T Inc. (T) and EchoStar Corporation (SATS) involved twice as much spectrum as Sirius XM’s 25 MHz and had a price tag of $23 billion. The other EchoStar transaction with SpaceX also involved 50 MHz and cost $19 billion. However, this can only serve as a rough goalpost, as the value of spectrum is determined by many different factors.

Clearly, the $8.6 billion carrying value was already justified by the current resulting earning power of the asset. Sirius XM will probably extract some $1.6 billion of unlevered FCF from its activities in 2026, and its enterprise value is just 11.6 times as much at $18.6 billion.

Any additional dollar of earnings should increment its valuation—even more so as it has been treated as a dying business.

Even without knowing exactly how much the potential new “space gatekeeper” business might add to the bottom line, probably the main takeaway for investors from this new regulatory proposal is that Sirius XM has growth options. It is not dying.

By the way, yesterday's announcement of an exclusive advertising partnership with YouTube highlights that industry experts perfectly recognize Sirius XM's market-leading position. The agreement makes SiriusXM Media the exclusive advertising representative of YouTube audio advertising inventory in the U.S.

Once at least some growth is factored in, $10 billion of debt is not an issue anymore. Shorts will cover. Cash will be available for buybacks and even higher dividends instead of debt reduction. A higher stock market valuation might lead to index inclusion and forced index fund buying.

Moreover, Sirius XM could be acquired after September 2026, when some provisions resulting from the Liberty Media merger expire.

For a long time, Sirius XM stock has suffered from a slowly declining business, which casts a long shadow over its many interesting assets. Even the tiniest bit of growth can suddenly and completely invert the picture.

data.title: BUZZ Investing: April Rebound Calms A March Washout
data.description:
U.S. equities sold off sharply through late March on geopolitical tensions and Fed caution, then rebounded in early April on stronger-than-expected jobs data and easing macro fears.

ImmunityBio (IBRX) entered at the maximum 3% weight on a social sentiment surge around its cancer immunotherapy ANKTIVA, joined by four crypto-related equities signaling a broad inflection in digital asset sentiment.

U.S. equity markets experienced continued volatility and a broad-based defensive retrenchment through much of the recent period between selection dates (March 12, 2026 – April 9, 2026, the “Period”), before staging a sharp recovery in the opening days of April that lifted the major indices back into positive territory. The opening phase extended the risk-off dynamics that had taken hold in early March, with the U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict showing no immediate signs of resolution. Elevated energy prices, still reflecting earlier disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, kept inflation concerns elevated, while the Federal Reserve’s March 18 meeting delivered little reassurance, holding the target upper bound of its policy rate at 3.75 percent and maintaining a cautious stance amid unresolved macro crosscurrents.

Investor positioning shifted decisively away from long-duration growth and AI-related equities, with software, semiconductor, and digital infrastructure names among the hardest hit as valuation support eroded and correlations spiked. By late March the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) had slipped into correction territory, and both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq had recorded intra-Period drawdowns of approximately 5 percent. Against this backdrop, the BUZZ NextGen AI US Sentiment Leaders Index (the “BUZZ Index”) proved especially vulnerable, selling off more than 10 percent during the Period as its higher-beta constituents participated disproportionately in the liquidation phase.

The tone improved materially in the first nine days of April, though the macro backdrop had not fully normalized. Incremental de-escalation signals in the geopolitical narrative, bolstered by improving negotiation headlines out of Washington and the region, helped ease tail risks and supported a rebound in risk appetite. This was reinforced by a stronger-than-expected March nonfarm payrolls report, which showed an increase of 178,000 jobs and the unemployment rate edging down to 4.3 percent, countering late-March fears of rapid economic deterioration. Investors also looked through a hotter March inflation print, interpreting it as transitory in light of resilient underlying demand. At the same time, fresh evidence of durable commercial traction across parts of the AI complex, particularly in enterprise applications and infrastructure, helped restore selective confidence in growth-oriented leadership.

By April 9, the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite recovered sufficiently to finish the Period in positive territory, up 2.4 percent and 2.3 percent, respectively. The BUZZ Index lagged the broader rebound, closing down 2.7 percent, reflecting the fact that many of its constituents had borne the brunt of the earlier drawdown and only partially recouped losses as sentiment stabilized. While the Period ended on a constructive note, underlying macro crosscurrents including geopolitical uncertainty, inflation vigilance, and Fed caution, remained in place, leaving the market’s conviction still somewhat tentative.

The BUZZ Index returned -6.22% during the month of March compared to a return of -4.98% for the S&P 500 Index during the same period. Year-to-date, the BUZZ Index lags the S&P 500 with returns of -11.21% and -4.33%, respectively, as of the end of March.

Nebius, Intel, and AMD Lead BUZZ Gains on AI Infrastructure Momentum

Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) was the leading contributor to BUZZ Index performance during the Period, marking its second consecutive appearance among the top contributors. The continued strength reflected sustained investor interest in companies viewed as credible beneficiaries of the AI infrastructure buildout, particularly where capital access, contracted demand, and expansion plans appeared tangible. Following the Nvidia (NVDA) investment and strategic validation discussed in the prior Period, Nebius remained well supported as investors absorbed its March agreement to provide Meta with up to $27 billion of AI computing capacity and its subsequent announcement of a 310-megawatt data center project in Finland, which would rank among Europe’s largest. Together, those developments reinforced the view that Nebius is evolving into a more scaled infrastructure platform with visible growth potential.

Intel Corporation (INTC) and Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) were also among the leading contributors during the Period, as investors rotated back into semiconductor names viewed as beneficiaries of sustained AI infrastructure spending. Intel’s advance was supported by several company-specific developments late in the Period, including its agreement to repurchase Apollo’s stake in its Ireland fabrication facility, its participation in Elon Musk’s Terafab initiative, and an expanded partnership with Google focused on AI and cloud infrastructure. AMD also participated in the rebound as confidence improved around the durability of AI-related capital spending and the company’s position within that ecosystem. During the Period, AMD announced an expanded strategic partnership with Samsung (SSNLF) around AI memory and manufacturing, while continued investor focus on its large-scale supply relationships with customers such as Meta (META) supported the broader view that AMD remains one of the few scaled alternatives in high-performance computing.

Source: BUZZ Holdings ULC, Bloomberg. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

Super Micro and Hims & Hers Health Lead BUZZ Declines on Company-Specific Setbacks

Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI) was the largest detractor from BUZZ Index performance during the Period, declining sharply after U.S. prosecutors charged the company’s co-founder, Yih-Shyan Liaw, along with two others, in connection with an alleged scheme to smuggle American-made AI servers containing restricted Nvidia chips into China in violation of U.S. export controls. Although Super Micro was not itself charged, the development revived broader concerns around governance, compliance, and customer trust at a company that had already faced heightened scrutiny over internal controls and accounting matters. The company responded by placing implicated individuals on leave, removing Liaw from the board, elevating a new chief compliance officer, and later launching an independent probe, but those steps did little to stabilize sentiment during the Period. The severity of the stock’s decline reflected not only the legal overhang, but also investor concern that the episode could disrupt commercial momentum or invite further regulatory scrutiny at a time when Super Micro had been trying to re-establish credibility as a key beneficiary of AI infrastructure spending.

Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (HIMS) was another notable detractor during the Period as investor skepticism continued to build around the durability and profitability of its weight-loss business. The stock remained under pressure as analysts reassessed the company’s 2026 earnings outlook considering an expected transition away from lower-cost compounded GLP-1 offerings toward branded products, a shift viewed as likely to compress margins and complicate subscriber conversion. That concern came against an already fragile backdrop following the FDA’s earlier crackdown on copycat GLP-1 products and growing questions around whether Hims can diversify quickly enough to offset pressure on its U.S. weight-loss franchise. While management has argued that the business can adapt and broaden beyond GLP-1s over time, the market appeared unconvinced during the Period, particularly as competition intensified and investors weighed the risk that growth may slow more sharply than previously expected.

BUZZ Index April 2026 Rebalance Highlights

ImmunityBio Inc. (IBRX)

The largest addition to the BUZZ Index in April is a biotech company, ImmunityBio (IBRX). The company’s lead product, ANKTIVA, is a non-invasive immunotherapy for bladder cancer. Earlier this year, ImmunityBio released a series of positive headlines, including stronger-than-expected ANKTIVA sales, encouraging clinical trial results, and several regulatory approvals in international markets. At the same time, the FDA’s review of the company’s plans to expand ANKTIVA’s label appeared to be progressing well. This steady stream of positive developments helped fuel a sharp squeeze in the stock, sending shares higher by roughly 500% by the end of February. The story quickly gained traction across online platforms, with trading volumes surging as investors debated the company’s broader potential. Investor enthusiasm was further amplified by ambitious comments from Executive Chairman Patrick Soon-Shiong, who suggested ANKTIVA could function as a “cancer vaccine” for “all cancers”. However, that narrative was challenged in March when the FDA issued a warning letter regarding the claim, resulting in a sharp pullback in the stock. Despite the subsequent decline, the surge in investor sentiment and volume of conversation has propelled IBRX into the BUZZ Index this month at the maximum 3% weight.

Crypto-Related Equities

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has retreated in recent months from its all-time highs, dragging down crypto-related companies ranging from digital asset treasuries (DATs), to miners, to digital transaction platforms. Despite this pullback, investor sentiment on the space has remained constructive over the past six months. Notably, this month saw a broad-based sentiment jump in crypto-related equities, resulting in four new crypto entrants into the BUZZ Index for April. The largest addition is CRCL (CRCL), the stablecoin issuer that went public last June, entering with a 1.08% weight. Close behind is TeraWulf (WULF), a clean-energy-focused Bitcoin miner, at 1.01%. BitMine Immersion Technologies (BMNR), a DAT positioning itself as the Ethereum version of Michael Saylor’s Strategy (MSTR), joins as a first-time constituent with a 0.95% weight. Finally, Galaxy Digital (GLXY) enters with a 0.66% weight. Founded by Mike Novogratz, GLXY operates a more institutionally oriented platform spanning trading, custody, staking, and asset management services. We view the sharp inflection in sentiment across crypto equities this month as notable and may signal an emerging turning point for the industry.
